reasoning:
To resolve YES, the number of protests in the 30-day window preceding each resolution date must exceed the 30-day average across the entire preceding 360 days (evaluated at the forecast date). Protest event data is strictly non-negative and highly right-skewed—most days/weeks see zero or few protests, while specific trigger events (e.g., the February 2025 anti-government rallies or August 2025 Gaza protests) cause massive localized spikes. Because the mean is pulled upwards by these clustered spikes, the vast majority of randomly selected 30-day periods will experience a protest count below the annual average (i.e., the median is typically lower than the mean).

Furthermore, the first two resolution periods (late November to late December 2025, and late December 2025 to late January 2026) coincide with the deep winter and the holiday season. Protest activity in European countries with cold winters, such as Slovenia, tends to be highly seasonal, significantly decreasing during December and January. Thus, the probability of exceeding the annual average in these windows is lower than an arbitrary month. 

The third period (late February to late March 2026) overlaps with the beginning of spring, a more common time for civic mobilization in the region. Correspondingly, the likelihood of exceeding the mean increases, but remains bounded below 50% due to the fundamental right-skewness of event distributions (spikes pull up the average, making it hard to beat on a random month). The provided reference value of '0.000' is likely due to an ACLED publishing lag, which will adjust upwards upon data reconciliation, restoring the typical statistical distribution constraints.
